_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan (考研) seven-select (7 choose 5) reading sets. 100% original, not copied from any real exam. Each passage 250-340 words with 5 numbered blanks (1)-(5); 7 full-sentence bank options (5 correct + 2 distractors); answers map gaps 1-5 to bank indices, distinct and within 0-6. Academic/abstract register, sophisticated discourse markers, unique-fit per gap locked by cohesion.

sets.passage: Few developments have unsettled the scientific community in recent decades as profoundly as the so-called replication crisis, a controversy that has forced entire disciplines to re-examine the very foundations on which their reputations rest. (1)______ When several teams of independent researchers systematically attempted to reproduce a large sample of celebrated psychological studies, a startling proportion of the original effects simply evaporated under renewed scrutiny, leaving behind little more than statistical noise and a lingering sense of unease. The reasons for this widespread failure are neither simple nor solely the product of deliberate dishonesty on the part of individual scientists. (2)______ Instead, much of the trouble can be traced to a tangle of ordinary institutional incentives that quietly reward novelty over reliability and spectacle over rigour. Academic journals, after all, generally prefer to publish surprising positive results rather than careful confirmations of what was already suspected to be true. (3)______ Under such relentless professional pressure, even scrupulous investigators may unconsciously analyze their data in whatever manner happens to yield the most publishable outcome, a questionable practice that researchers now euphemistically describe as analytical flexibility. The consequences of all this extend well beyond mere academic embarrassment within the ivory tower. (4)______ Clinical recommendations, educational policies, and ambitious public-health campaigns are frequently built upon empirical findings that, it later quietly emerges, virtually no one is able to repeat. Encouragingly, the crisis has also provoked a vigorous wave of methodological reform across the sciences. (5)______ By specifying their hypotheses and their precise analytical procedures in advance, scientists make it far harder to disguise a lucky statistical accident as a genuine discovery, and the slow, unglamorous labour of replication is gradually being restored to its rightful place at the very heart of the scientific enterprise.
bank: Researchers are increasingly required to register their study designs publicly before any data are collected.
The term refers to the unsettling discovery that many published findings cannot be reproduced by other laboratories.
A doctoral candidate who fails to produce striking effects may struggle to secure a position at all.
Outright fraud, while it certainly occurs, accounts for only a small fraction of the irreproducible literature.
Most scientists, by contrast, regard peer review as an infallible guarantee of truth.
Patients and policymakers, who rarely read the original papers, inherit conclusions that rest on shaky foundations.
Statistical significance has therefore become the single most trustworthy measure of scientific merit.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选B（the so-called replication crisis 之后用 The term refers to... 定义该术语，指代衔接）；空2选D（neither simply... 之后用 Outright fraud... accounts for only a small fraction 排除欺诈这一单一原因，逻辑递进）；空3选C（journals prefer surprising results 之后举博士生若拿不出惊人结果则难就业，example 衔接，且引出后句 Under such pressure）；空4选F（consequences extend beyond academic embarrassment 之后说患者与决策者继承了不牢靠的结论，具体化后果）；空5选A（a wave of methodological reform 之后举预注册研究设计这一具体改革，且后句 By specifying their hypotheses in advance 与 register their study designs 形成词汇链）。干扰句E（同行评审万无一失）、G（统计显著性是最可信指标）与全文反讽语气矛盾，放入任一空都与论点冲突，不连贯。

sets.passage: We tend instinctively to picture human memory as a kind of vast personal archive, an orderly warehouse in which the countless events of our lives are faithfully filed away and later retrieved entirely intact upon demand. (1)______ Modern cognitive science, however, has over the past few decades thoroughly dismantled this comfortable and intuitive metaphor. Far from passively replaying a fixed recording, the brain actively reconstructs each memory afresh every single time it is summoned, hastily stitching together scattered fragments of sensation, expectation, and inference into something that merely feels seamless and whole. (2)______ This reconstructive activity is precisely why two people can witness the very same incident at the very same moment yet later recall it in mutually incompatible ways, each utterly and sincerely convinced of the perfect accuracy of their own version. The reconstructive nature of recollection naturally carries some genuinely unsettling implications for everyday life. (3)______ In a now celebrated line of experiments, ordinary participants were gradually induced to remember vivid childhood episodes that had never once actually taken place, simply because a trusted authority figure suggested those events with sufficient calm confidence. Such striking findings have profound and troubling consequences for the operation of the justice system. (4)______ Eyewitness testimony, long regarded by courts as among the most persuasive forms of evidence available, turns out on closer inspection to be alarmingly malleable, dangerously vulnerable to the leading questions of an interviewer or the contaminating reports of fellow witnesses. None of this, it must be stressed, means that memory is somehow worthless or untrustworthy. (5)______ A faculty that rigidly prized literal accuracy over flexible meaning would have been almost useless to a creature that must constantly anticipate an uncertain future rather than merely catalogue a vanished past.
bank: On this view, remembering is less an act of retrieval than an act of creative assembly.
Indeed, the very plasticity that makes our recollections fallible is what allows us to learn and to imagine.
It implies, most strikingly, that entirely false memories can be implanted with disturbing ease.
Few of us are willing to abandon so intuitive and reassuring an account of our own minds.
Photographic recall, the studies confirm, is the natural default state of the healthy human brain.
Jurors place enormous trust in witnesses who recount events with vivid and confident detail.
Such confidence, regrettably, bears almost no reliable relationship to the truth of what is recalled.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选D（picture memory as an archive 之后用 Few of us are willing to abandon so intuitive... an account 承接这一直觉看法，且引出后句 however 的转折）；空2选A（reconstructs each memory afresh 之后用 On this view, remembering is... an act of creative assembly 概括重构观点，指代衔接 this view）；空3选C（unsettling implications 之后用 It implies... that false memories can be implanted 点明含义，后句 In a celebrated line of experiments 举证）；空4选F（consequences for the justice system 之后说陪审员高度信任自信的证人，具体化司法影响，引出后句 Eyewitness testimony）；空5选B（None of this means memory is worthless 之后用 Indeed, the very plasticity... allows us to learn 转折肯定其价值，且 plasticity 与后句的 flexible meaning 形成词汇链）。干扰句E（照相式记忆是大脑默认状态）、G（自信与真实毫无关系——E与全文矛盾，G缺乏对应指代空位且会与F重复证人话题但断裂）放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Each new year reliably brings a fresh torrent of confident forecasts from economists, political analysts, and assorted technology gurus, and each year a strikingly large number of those very predictions prove, in due course, to be embarrassingly and even spectacularly wrong. (1)______ The psychologist Philip Tetlock patiently documented this dispiriting pattern across two decades of meticulous research, finding that the average credentialed expert performed scarcely better than random guessing whenever projecting political and economic events more than a year or two ahead. (2)______ Curiously and rather tellingly, the celebrated pundits who commanded the greatest share of media fame were frequently among the very least accurate forecasters of the entire sample. The explanation for these persistent failures lies partly in the irreducible nature of genuinely complex systems. (3)______ Economies, ecosystems, and entire societies are governed by countless interacting variables whose tiny initial fluctuations can amplify over time into vast and wholly unforeseeable outcomes, rendering most long-range forecasts little better than educated guesswork. Yet the failure of expert prediction is also, in large measure, deeply psychological in origin. (4)______ Experts tend to grow emotionally attached to grand unifying theories that conveniently compress a messy and contradictory world into a single tidy story, and they cling stubbornly to these cherished frameworks even as disconfirming evidence steadily accumulates around them. Tetlock did, however, manage to identify a small minority of thinkers who consistently outperformed their peers. (5)______ Rather than zealously defending sweeping ideologies, these so-called superforecasters treated every prediction as a strictly provisional estimate, eagerly revising their stated views whenever fresh information arrived, however inconvenient or unwelcome it happened to prove.
bank: The mind, it turns out, recoils from admitting its own uncertainty far more than mere logic alone would dictate.
These individuals shared not superior knowledge but a distinctive cast of mind.
Greater confidence, his data showed, tended to correlate with greater fame rather than greater precision.
The reliability of expert forecasting, in short, is far lower than its prestige would suggest.
The deeper one's expertise, the more reliably accurate one's long-term predictions become.
Small initial differences, as chaos theory long ago revealed, can produce wildly divergent futures.
Television audiences naturally prefer cautious hedging to bold and dramatic pronouncements.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选D（forecasts prove embarrassingly wrong 之后用 The reliability of expert forecasting... is far lower than its prestige 总括论点，且后句 The psychologist Philip Tetlock documented this pattern 中 this pattern 指代该论点）；空2选C（Tetlock 发现专家不比瞎猜强 之后用 Greater confidence... correlate with greater fame rather than precision 给出数据，且后句 Curiously, the pundits with the greatest media fame were least accurate 与之呼应）；空3选F（the nature of complex systems 之后用 Small initial differences... can produce wildly divergent futures 引混沌理论解释复杂系统，后句 Economies, ecosystems 进一步展开）；空4选A（the failure is also psychological in origin 之后用 The mind recoils from admitting its own uncertainty far more than logic would dictate 直接承接心理成因、无悬空指代，且后句 Experts grow emotionally attached to grand theories and cling stubbornly 正是该心智抗拒承认不确定的展开）；空5选B（identify a minority who outperformed 之后用 These individuals shared... a distinctive cast of mind 指代该少数人，且后句 Rather than defending ideologies 展开其特质）。干扰句E（专业越深预测越准）、G（电视观众偏爱谨慎对冲）与全文论点矛盾，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Popular accounts of science very often portray it as a steady and orderly march toward an ever-clearer picture of objective reality, with each successive generation simply adding fresh bricks to a sturdy wall first begun by its diligent predecessors. (1)______ The influential historian Thomas Kuhn forcefully challenged this tidy and flattering image, arguing instead that science actually lurches forward through long periods of relative calm that are punctuated by sudden and dramatic upheavals. During the extended stretches of what he memorably termed normal science, researchers work contentedly within a single accepted framework, patiently refining its finer details and methodically solving its outstanding puzzles. (2)______ Troublesome anomalies that stubbornly resist explanation are at first dismissed as minor irritants, quietly set aside in the comfortable hope that some clever future adjustment will eventually accommodate them. Over a sufficient span of time, however, these stubborn and unresolved exceptions may slowly accumulate until the prevailing framework itself begins to creak audibly under their mounting weight. (3)______ A genuine crisis duly ensues, and the wider research community gradually grows newly receptive to the very radical alternatives that would earlier have been dismissed or openly ridiculed. When a fresh framework finally triumphs over the old, the resulting transformation can be startlingly and almost disorientingly complete. (4)______ Phenomena once deemed absolutely central are suddenly rendered trivial, questions once thought wholly meaningless abruptly become urgent, and the very technical vocabulary of the field is quietly rewritten from the ground up. Critics have nonetheless charged that this whole account makes scientific progress sound alarmingly irrational, a mere matter of intellectual fashion rather than sober evidence. (5)______ The accumulated weight of stubborn anomalies, and not any mere caprice, is ultimately what topples an exhausted framework and clears the ground for its eventual successor.
bank: Such revolutions do not merely add new facts; they reorganize the entire landscape of inquiry.
Defenders reply, with considerable force, that the upheavals are driven by genuine empirical pressure.
The reigning paradigm, in other words, dictates which questions are worth asking and which may be ignored.
Yet the actual history of inquiry rarely conforms to this reassuringly cumulative narrative.
Scientific theories, once firmly established, are essentially never overturned by later generations.
Eventually no amount of patching can preserve the credibility of the old way of seeing things.
Most scientists welcome such crises as exhilarating opportunities to abandon their cherished assumptions.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选D（portray science as a steady march 之后用 Yet the actual history... rarely conforms to this cumulative narrative 转折否定该图景，this narrative 指代前句的累积叙事，且后句 Thomas Kuhn challenged this tidy image 呼应）；空2选C（work within an accepted framework 之后用 The reigning paradigm... dictates which questions are worth asking 阐释范式作用，后句 Anomalies... dismissed 是其结果）；空3选F（stubborn exceptions accumulate 之后用 Eventually no amount of patching can preserve the old way 说明修补失效，后句 A crisis ensues 承接）；空4选A（the transformation can be startlingly complete 之后用 Such revolutions... reorganize the entire landscape 指代该剧变，后句 Phenomena once central are suddenly trivial 举例）；空5选B（critics charged it sounds irrational 之后用 Defenders reply... driven by genuine empirical pressure 反驳，后句 The accumulated weight of anomalies, not mere caprice 与 empirical pressure 形成语义链）。干扰句E（理论一旦确立永不被推翻）、G（多数科学家欢迎危机去抛弃信念）与库恩论点及人性描写矛盾，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Why exactly do ordinary people fret so anxiously about exotic and dramatic dangers that almost never actually materialize, while casually shrugging off the mundane everyday hazards that quietly kill them by the thousands every year? (1)______ The growing discipline of risk psychology has demonstrated, again and again, that our intuitive human sense of danger bears only a surprisingly loose relationship to genuine statistical probability. A single vivid plane crash will dominate the international headlines for weeks on end, whereas the far deadlier daily toll exacted by routine road accidents passes almost entirely unremarked. (2)______ Because such dramatic events are simply far easier to recall vividly, the mind unconsciously mistakes their mental availability for their real frequency, thereby systematically overestimating the rare and the spectacular. Several other powerful cognitive biases compound and deepen this fundamental distortion. (3)______ A threat that we happen to choose entirely voluntarily, such as smoking or mountaineering, characteristically feels far more acceptable to us than one that is imposed upon us from outside, even when the imposed risk is objectively trivial by comparison. The peculiar emotion of dread plays its own significant part as well. (4)______ Hazards that strike invisibly and that inspire grim images of slow, lingering suffering, such as radiation or chemical contamination, reliably provoke a public fear out of all proportion to the modest harm they actually cause. Understanding these underlying psychological mechanisms is by no means merely an idle academic exercise. (5)______ When harried policymakers allocate genuinely scarce resources according to the pitch of public alarm rather than according to measured evidence, they may pour entire fortunes into preventing improbable catastrophes while badly neglecting the quiet, unglamorous epidemics that claim far more lives.
bank: The answer, researchers argue, lies in a collection of predictable mental shortcuts.
One of the most powerful of these is the availability heuristic.
The character of a risk, it emerges, matters as much to us as its sheer magnitude.
Misperceived risk can warp public spending in genuinely costly ways.
Human beings, on the whole, assess danger with cool and dispassionate accuracy.
We dread some harms far more than others for reasons that have little to do with body counts.
Familiarity with a hazard, oddly enough, makes people demand ever stricter regulation of it.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选A（开篇设问 Why do people fret... 之后用 The answer... lies in a collection of mental shortcuts 直接作答，且后句 risk psychology 与 mental shortcuts 呼应）；空2选B（plane crash vs road accidents 之后用 One of the most powerful of these is the availability heuristic 点名机制，these 指代前句 mental shortcuts，后句 Because dramatic events are easier to recall 解释该启发式）；空3选C（Several other biases 之后用 The character of a risk matters as much as its magnitude 引出风险性质这一维度，后句 A threat we choose voluntarily 是其例证）；空4选F（Dread plays its part 之后用 We dread some harms far more than others 承接 dread，后句 Hazards that strike invisibly 展开）；空5选D（not merely an academic exercise 之后用 Misperceived risk can warp public spending 说明现实意义，后句 When policymakers allocate resources 展开）。干扰句E（人类冷静准确评估风险）、G（熟悉反而要求更严监管）与全文论点相反，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: In a noisy modern age that loudly prizes unwavering confidence and decisive certainty above almost everything else, the quiet and unfashionable virtue of intellectual humility is all too easily mistaken for mere weakness or indecision. (1)______ Yet the simple willingness to acknowledge honestly the limits of one's own knowledge may well be among the most genuinely valuable dispositions that any serious thinking person can hope to cultivate over a lifetime. Intellectual humility, it must be emphasised, does not in the slightest entail timidity, passivity, or perpetual paralysing self-doubt. (2)______ Rather, it means holding all of one's beliefs strictly in proportion to the available evidence, remaining genuinely open to revision when warranted, and steadfastly resisting the seductive emotional comfort of premature certainty. Such a demanding intellectual stance, unfortunately, runs sharply against the grain of ordinary human nature. (3)______ We appear to be psychologically wired to defend our cherished convictions as though they were literal extensions of our own selves, instinctively treating any pointed challenge to a favoured idea as a direct assault upon our very identity. The practical benefits of consciously overcoming this deep-seated tendency, however, are genuinely considerable and well documented. (4)______ People who can sincerely entertain the live possibility that they are mistaken tend to learn faster, to collaborate far more effectively with others, and to be markedly less likely to be ensnared and trapped by their own accumulating errors. Crucially, humility of precisely this kind can be deliberately practised and trained rather than merely admired wistfully from afar. (5)______ By habitually seeking out the very views that contradict their own and by openly rewarding the changing of one's mind rather than treating it as a humiliating defeat, whole communities can slowly cultivate a culture in which being wrong becomes a welcome occasion for learning rather than for shame.
bank: Rather, it is a robust and demanding discipline of the mind.
The capacity to say I might be wrong is too often read as a failure of conviction.
Studies of reasoning repeatedly confirm the practical payoff of this open posture.
Individuals and institutions alike can adopt concrete habits that nurture it.
The greatest thinkers, after all, have always been those who refused ever to doubt themselves.
Our instinct is not to weigh ideas dispassionately but to protect those we already hold.
That earlier objection, by contrast, collapses the moment one concedes that arrogance learns nothing at all.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选B（mistaken for weakness 之后用 The capacity to say I might be wrong is read as a failure of conviction 具体化这一误解，后句 Yet the willingness to acknowledge limits 转折）；空2选A（does not entail timidity or self-doubt 之后用 Rather, it is a robust and demanding discipline 用 Rather 形成对比，后句 It means holding beliefs in proportion to evidence 展开）；空3选F（runs against the grain of human nature 之后用 Our instinct is not to weigh ideas... but to protect those we already hold 阐释人性，后句 We are wired to defend our convictions 平行强化）；空4选C（The benefits... are considerable 之后用 Studies of reasoning confirm the practical payoff 给出证据，this open posture 指代前文谦逊姿态，后句 People who entertain being mistaken 举例）；空5选D（can be deliberately practised 之后用 Individuals and institutions can adopt concrete habits that nurture it 承接可操作性，it 指代该谦逊，后句 By habitually seeking out contradicting views 给出方法）。干扰句E（伟大思想家从不怀疑自己）与全文倡导矛盾；G（That earlier objection, by contrast... 带悬空指示词 That earlier objection 且 by contrast 衔接无对应前文）指代落空、连接词无槽位匹配，放入任一空都断裂不连贯。

sets.passage: Does the particular language that we happen to speak quietly shape the very way in which we think about the world around us? The old question, once airily dismissed by many scholars as romantic nonsense, has lately enjoyed a remarkably sober and rigorous scientific revival. (1)______ The strongest and boldest version of the idea, which once held that language rigidly imprisons and constrains all thought, has by now been largely abandoned, for speakers plainly manage to grasp concepts for which their native tongue lacks any single convenient word to name. A far subtler and more cautious hypothesis, however, has so far survived repeated rigorous experimental testing. (2)______ On this account, language does not strictly determine the boundaries of what we are able to think, but it gently nudges and steers what we habitually notice, attend to, and later remember. Consider, by way of illustration, the everyday treatment of physical space. (3)______ Speakers of certain Aboriginal Australian languages, which describe location only in absolute compass directions rather than in relative egocentric terms like left and right, reliably develop a remarkable and almost constant awareness of their orientation that ordinary English speakers seldom if ever possess. The perception of colour offers a second instructive illustration of the same principle. (4)______ Communities whose everyday vocabulary draws a sharp lexical boundary between two neighbouring shades can discriminate between those shades measurably faster than communities whose language simply lumps the pair together under one umbrella term. Such intriguing findings should, nevertheless, be handled and interpreted with considerable care. (5)______ The observed effects, while genuinely real and reliably replicable, are typically rather modest in scale, chiefly influencing the speed and ease of certain mental operations rather than imposing the hard cognitive limits that the bolder early theorists once confidently imagined.
bank: It proposes that language exerts a gentle but measurable influence upon perception and habit.
Decades of evidence have steadily narrowed the range of defensible positions.
Where a language compels its users to attend to direction, that attention becomes second nature.
Here too, the grammar of a tongue appears to sharpen or blur the distinctions its speakers make.
Thought, the experiments prove conclusively, is wholly independent of the words available to express it.
It would be a mistake to exaggerate the magnitude of these linguistic influences.
Bilingual speakers, notably, are entirely unable to perceive any colour their first language ignores.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选B（the question enjoyed a scientific revival 之后用 Decades of evidence have narrowed the range of defensible positions 总括研究进展，后句 The strongest version... has been abandoned 是narrowing的具体表现）；空2选A（A subtler hypothesis has survived 之后用 It proposes that language exerts a gentle but measurable influence 阐释该假说，It 指代 hypothesis，后句 Language does not determine but nudges 同义展开）；空3选C（Consider the treatment of space 之后用 Where a language compels users to attend to direction, attention becomes second nature 给出空间方面的原理，后句 Speakers of certain Aboriginal languages 举例）；空4选D（Colour offers a second illustration 之后用 Here too, the grammar... sharpen or blur distinctions 用 Here too 承接到颜色，后句 Communities whose vocabulary draws a sharp boundary 举例）；空5选F（should be handled with care 之后用 It would be a mistake to exaggerate the magnitude 承接审慎告诫，后句 The effects... are typically modest 展开）。干扰句E（思维完全独立于语言）、G（双语者无法感知母语忽略的颜色）走极端，与全文 subtle/modest 立场矛盾，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Scientific misinformation now travels through the global internet with a speed, reach, and stubborn persistence that genuine, carefully verified findings consistently struggle to match. (1)______ A single misleading claim about the safety of vaccines or the reality of climate change can circle the entire globe several times over before careful, conscientious researchers have even finished drafting a single measured and properly qualified rebuttal. This troubling asymmetry between lies and truth is by no means accidental or coincidental. (2)______ Falsehoods are frequently crafted, whether deliberately or not, to be simple, emotionally charged, and reassuringly certain, whereas authentic science is necessarily hedged with cautious qualifications and burdened by inconvenient layers of complexity. This fundamental mismatch is then dramatically amplified and accelerated by the underlying architecture of the major social media platforms. (3)______ Algorithms carefully tuned above all to maximize user engagement will reliably promote whatever content happens to provoke the strongest emotional reaction, and naked outrage predictably spreads far more briskly through a network than careful nuance ever possibly could. Worse still, well-meaning attempts to correct a popular falsehood can on occasion paradoxically entrench it more deeply. (4)______ Loudly repeating a myth in order to debunk it may inadvertently increase its sheer familiarity in people's minds, and familiar claims tend to feel intuitively truer regardless of their actual accuracy. None of this discouraging picture, however, ultimately justifies fatalism or despair. (5)______ Patiently teaching people to recognize the common rhetorical tactics of manipulation in advance, before they ever encounter a specific lie, has proved considerably more durable and effective than laboriously correcting falsehoods after the unfortunate fact, much as a vaccine prepares the body before any infection actually arrives.
bank: Researchers have nonetheless identified strategies that genuinely help.
Part of the problem lies in the very content of the false messages themselves.
The platforms, after all, profit from attention rather than from accuracy.
The phenomenon arises partly from the basic economics of human attention.
Accurate scientific information, fortunately, always spreads faster than any rumour.
This counterintuitive backfire effect has been documented in numerous studies.
Audiences, it should be stressed, can always be relied upon to distinguish fact from fiction.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选D（misinformation travels faster than findings 之后用 The phenomenon arises partly from the basic economics of human attention 点出成因，后句 A single misleading claim can circle the globe 是该现象的写照）；空2选B（The asymmetry is not accidental 之后用 Part of the problem lies in the content of the false messages 指出内容层面成因，后句 Falsehoods are crafted to be simple 展开）；空3选C（amplified by the architecture of social platforms 之后用 The platforms profit from attention rather than accuracy 解释平台动机，后句 Algorithms tuned to maximize engagement 展开）；空4选F（attempts to correct can entrench it 之后用 This counterintuitive backfire effect has been documented 命名该效应，This 指代前句的反效果，后句 Repeating a myth to debunk it 解释机制）；空5选A（None of this justifies despair 之后用 Researchers have identified strategies that genuinely help 转向解决方案，后句 Teaching people to recognize tactics 是具体策略）。干扰句E（准确信息总比谣言传得快）、G（受众总能辨别真伪）与全文论点相反，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: The popular and enduring image of scientific discovery characteristically features a lone, brooding genius reasoning methodically and inexorably toward some single predetermined goal. (1)______ The actual historical record, by sharp contrast, is generously studded with happy accidents, unexpected lucky breaks, and curious observations that absolutely no one had been looking for in the first place. Life-saving penicillin famously emerged from a contaminated, mouldy culture dish that a slightly tidier and more fastidious researcher might well have simply thrown straight into the bin. (2)______ The modern microwave oven, in much the same accidental fashion, was born when an alert engineer happened to notice that a chocolate bar had quietly melted in his trouser pocket while he stood near an active radar device. Yet it would be a serious mistake to dismiss such celebrated episodes as nothing more than sheer dumb good fortune. (3)______ Alexander Fleming recognized the profound significance of his contaminated dish precisely because long years of disciplined and patient study had carefully prepared his mind to interpret the strange anomaly correctly. As Louis Pasteur himself famously observed long ago, chance favours only the prepared mind. (4)______ A rigid laboratory that harshly punishes every small deviation from the planned protocol, or a narrow funding system that arrogantly demands a guaranteed outcome before any money at all is released, may quite unwittingly strangle the genuinely unexpected discovery at its very birth. This whole pattern suggests a distinctly paradoxical practical lesson for those who fund and manage scientific research. (5)______ The wisest institutions therefore deliberately protect a generous measure of unstructured time and patiently tolerate the apparent waste of numerous exploratory dead ends, knowing full well that the most transformative findings can seldom if ever be reliably ordered in advance.
bank: Serendipity, in short, rewards preparation rather than replacing it.
It depicts a process that is orderly, deliberate, and almost entirely free of surprise.
Such accidental breakthroughs are far more common in the history of science than the textbooks admit.
The conditions that allow serendipity to flourish can, moreover, be encouraged or suppressed.
True breakthroughs, the evidence shows, arrive strictly according to carefully laid plans.
Productivity cannot be maximized by allowing scientists to wander without clear objectives.
One cannot schedule a happy accident, yet one can create the conditions in which accidents become fruitful.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选B（image of a lone genius reasoning methodically 之后用 It depicts a process that is orderly and free of surprise 补足该形象，It 指代前句图景，后句 The actual record, by contrast 转折）；空2选C（Penicillin from a mouldy dish 之后用 Such accidental breakthroughs are far more common than textbooks admit 概括偶然突破，后句 The microwave oven was born when... 再举一例）；空3选A（a mistake to dismiss as mere good fortune 之后用 Serendipity rewards preparation rather than replacing it 点明偶然需准备，后句 Fleming recognized the significance because years of study had prepared him 是其证明）；空4选D（chance favours the prepared mind 之后用 The conditions that allow serendipity can be encouraged or suppressed 转向制度条件，后句 A laboratory that punishes deviation 举抑制之例）；空5选G（a paradoxical lesson for those who manage research 之后用 One cannot schedule a happy accident, yet one can create the conditions 给出悖论式启示，后句 The wisest institutions protect unstructured time 展开）。干扰句E（突破严格按计划到来）、F（不能让科学家无目标漫游来最大化产出）与全文论点矛盾，放入任一空都不连贯。

sets.passage: The vast and ever-growing troves of personal data now routinely harvested by governments and large corporations alike have decisively outpaced our collective social capacity to reason clearly about their ethics. (1)______ Every casual click, every minor purchase, and every silent location ping leaves behind a small digital trace that, once carefully aggregated with thousands of others, can reveal intimate facts about a person that he or she never knowingly disclosed to anyone. Defenders of large-scale data collection are quick to point to its genuinely undeniable benefits for society. (2)______ Public-health authorities, for instance, can now track the spread of an epidemic almost in real time, and scientists can detect subtle statistical patterns that would remain entirely invisible to any single human observer. Yet these very real gains invariably arrive deeply entangled with a number of correspondingly serious hazards. (3)______ Information originally gathered for one ostensibly benign and limited purpose is routinely repurposed later for some quite different end to which the individual never actually agreed, a creeping practice that quietly erodes the entire meaning of informed consent. The conventional legal safeguard, namely asking users to agree formally to a lengthy privacy policy, offers in reality only the comforting illusion of genuine protection. (4)______ Few ordinary people ever read these notoriously dense documents, fewer still genuinely understand their dense provisions, and almost no one is realistically free to decline outright the essential services that they govern. Genuine and lasting reform will therefore require considerably more than merely longer and more detailed disclosure forms. (5)______ It demands binding rules that meaningfully constrain how data may be combined and reused down the line, quite regardless of whatever fine print a distracted user may once have clicked through in a single moment of haste.
bank: Aggregation, in particular, poses a danger that isolated data points do not.
The sheer scale of modern surveillance has no real precedent in human history.
Such consent, in practice, is rarely either informed or meaningfully voluntary.
Analysed responsibly, the same datasets can save lives and advance knowledge.
Most users, surveys show, read every privacy agreement in careful and complete detail.
The remedy must shift the burden from the individual to those who profit from the data.
Personal data, once collected, never poses any risk to the people it describes.
sets.explain_zh: 空1选B（data has outpaced our capacity to reason about ethics 之后用 The sheer scale of modern surveillance has no precedent 强调规模，后句 Every click leaves a trace that can reveal intimate facts 是规模的体现）；空2选D（Defenders point to undeniable benefits 之后用 Analysed responsibly, the same datasets can save lives and advance knowledge 概括益处，后句 Public-health authorities can track an epidemic 举例）；空3选A（these gains arrive entangled with hazards 之后用 Aggregation poses a danger that isolated data points do not 点出聚合风险，后句 Information gathered for one purpose is repurposed for another 展开）；空4选C（privacy policy offers only the illusion of protection 之后用 Such consent is rarely informed or voluntary 解释同意为何无效，Such consent 指代前句的同意机制，后句 Few people read these documents 给出依据）；空5选F（reform requires more than longer forms 之后用 The remedy must shift the burden from the individual to those who profit 给出改革方向，后句 It demands rules that constrain how data may be combined 展开）。干扰句E（多数用户认真读完每份隐私协议）、G（个人数据一旦收集绝无风险）与全文论点相反，放入任一空都不连贯。
